---
title: "Module 9 Assignment"
description: |
  Introduction to  MySQL and connection to PHP
preview: https://github.com/skywalshUSF/LIS4365_Blog/blob/main/_posts/2026-03-02-module-8-assignment/form.png?raw=true
author:
  - name: Skylar Walsh
date: 2026-03-09
output:
  distill::distill_article:
    self_contained: false
editor_options: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE)
```

## These are responses to our module 9 tasks

In your blog, you will discuss the three steps you conducted in creating
your first MySQL table and explain what was easy, hard and not clear in
your progress in creating your first table in MySQL. Make sure to
provide detail explanation on each step.

## Step 1

In your infinityfree.net and your cpanel/Vistapanel. Click on Create New
MySQLdatabases.

To create a new MySQL database on infinityfree.com, I had to use their
web-based control panel. This means that I cannot directly use SQL
commands from within scripts or external tools. Following the steps to
create a database, I started by accessing my hosting account, then
opened the control panel. I then navigated to the databases section and
selected the MySQL databases icon. Finally, I began naming and creating
a database by entering a name using only letters and numbers. Once that
was created, I got these specific details to connect my website to the
database: the database name, the username, and the hostname.

## Step 2

Next, step II click on phpMyAdmin. It will open up the table. Here is
where you will design your own table properties. Your assignment is to
provide two variables. In this example, I create three variables. When
you create your variables, you need to decide on variable TYPE. Your
selection will consist of: INT, VARCHAR, TEXT, DATA, NUMERIC. Make sure
you fully understand what INT and VARCHAR stand for.

When I first opened phpMyAdmin and selected my database, I saw a screen
asking me to "Create table." Designing a table is basically defining the
columns or attributes that will hold my data, as well as rows that store
each record or entity instance. I started by naming my table
'module9assignment.' By giving my table a name, I am also giving my
entity or object a name. Next, I began entering properties for each
column. In that process, I made sure that each variable name for the row
has the right type to keep my database fast and organized. For instance,
INT is best for whole numbers, VARCHAR is best for variable-length text,
TEXT is best for content or descriptions that are long, DATE is good for
data in chronological order, and NUMERIC is good for any kind of number,
including whole numbers. Once I filled out my columns and rows, I
scrolled down and clicked Save, where my table is now live and ready to
receive data.

## Step 3

Click on the Insert Tab under PHPMyAdmin. In this Tab you will fill out
the data you assign to your variables.

Once I have created my table structure, adding actual data is done
through a manual entry form in the Insert Tab. In phpMyAdmin, I clicked
on the name of the database I created, and below the database name, a
list of my tables appeared. While I was doing this, I clicked the
specific table I want to fill out. Once I have filled in the fields, I
clicked the Go button and saw a green success message appear. Finally, I
clicked the Browse tab to view my newly added data.

Two queries were generated. The first was using the INSERT command.
Then, Query 2 used SELECTALL (which is used to select everything from a
table).

## Explain what was easy, hard, and not clear in your progress in creating your first table in MySQL.

This is my learning curve for my progress in creating my first table in
MySQL:

Starting with the easy stuff, creating the database is just a click of a
button in the InfinityFree dashboard, and it was the simplest task. Once
the table exists, the insert tab feels very familiar. Also, using
phpMyAdmin is much easier than typing raw SQL code into a black command
prompt.

For the hard stuff, choosing the right data types was hard. I picked INT
for one of my variables but tried to save a decimal, which made my data
break. Also, the fact that you cannot use external software on
InfinityFree makes debugging a bit more tedious since you are forced to
use the web interface.

Finally, for the unclear stuff, InfinityFree uses my Main Hosting
Account Password for the database, not a separate one I created. This
gives me "Access Denied" errors when I try to use a client area login
password instead.
